← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Islamic World

From Al-Andalus to Samarkand — geometry, calligraphy, tile, and dome.

Islamic art spans three continents and fourteen centuries. Without figural imagery at its centre, it found other languages: endless pattern, abstract color, script as image, and vast cool interiors that turn geometry into prayer.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

500 – 1000

Hagia Sophia
Byzantine & Ottoman · 537 CE onward · Istanbul
A Byzantine cathedral that held the largest dome on earth for a thousand years, then an Ottoman mosque, then a museum, now a mosque again.
Luxurious Woolen Tunic with Decorated Bands and Roundels
Egypt, Umayyad period (661–750) · 600s-700s · weft-faced plain weave with slit-and dovetailed-tapestry weave, supplementary weft wrapping, embroidery; undyed linen, dyed wool
Roundel, Segmentum from a Tunic
Egypt, Umayyad period (661–750) · mid 600s - mid 700s · tapestry; linen and wool
Fragment of a Band or Border
Egypt, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · late 600s–800s · Wool and linen: tapestry weave
Dome of the Rock
Umayyad · 691 CE · Jerusalem
The earliest surviving major Islamic building. The gold dome marks the rock from which Muhammad is said to have ascended on the Night Journey.
Decorations and Sleeve from a Tunic
Egypt, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · wool; plain weave with slit-tapestry weave
Clavi (Decorative Band)
Egypt, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · wool; plain weave with slit-tapestry weave
Sleeve from a Tunic
Egypt, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · wool; plain weave with slit-tapestry weave
Square Ornament from a Tunic
Egypt or Syria, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · Silk; complementary weft-faced twill weave with inner warps (samite)
Silk Sleeve Decoration with Hunters
Egypt or Syria, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · Silk: complementary weft-faced twill with inner warps (samit)
Nude Female Dancers from a Tunic
Egypt or Syria, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · Silk; complementary weft-faced twill weave with inner warps (samite)
Alexander the Great on Horseback
Egypt, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · dyed wool, undyed linen; plain weave and slit-tapestry weave
Silk Decorative Tunic Band with a Hunter
Egypt or Syria, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · Silk: complementary weft-faced twill with inner warps (samit)
Silk Fragments with Palmette Blossoms
Egypt or Syria, Umayyad period (661–750) or Abbasid period (750–1258) · 700s · Silk: complementary weft-faced twill with inner warps (samit)
Al-Aqsa Mosque
Umayyad onward · 705 CE · Jerusalem
The congregational mosque on the Temple Mount, third holiest site in Islam. Rebuilt repeatedly after earthquakes; the wooden prayer hall facing the qibla dates partly to the 1930s.
Umayyad Mosque of Damascus
Umayyad · 715 · Syria
Built on a site already sacred to Jupiter, John the Baptist, and Mary. Byzantine mosaicists were brought in to lay the gold-ground interior.
Qusayr 'Amra
Umayyad · c. 723 · Jordan
A desert bath complex covered inside with frescoes of wrestlers, bathing women, and the six kings of the world — Caesar, Negus, Khosrow — defeated or soon to be defeated by Islam.
Mshatta Facade
Umayyad · 743–744 · Jordan
A desert palace facade dismantled and shipped to Berlin in 1903 as a diplomatic gift. Carved rosettes and winged lions in the softest yellow sandstone; too brittle to move today.
Jameh Mosque of Isfahan
Seljuq onward · 8th c. – modern · Iran
A thousand years of Iranian architecture layered in one building — two of the greatest domes in Islamic history stand a few meters apart.
Khirbat al-Mafjar
Umayyad · 8th c. · Palestine
"Hisham's Palace" — an early Umayyad palace-bath complex with the single largest and most detailed mosaic floor of its era, a lion stalking gazelles beneath a tree of life.
Umayyad Clavi
Umayyad / early Abbasid · 8th c. · Egypt
A decorative tapestry-woven band ("clavus") from a Coptic-style tunic made under Muslim rule — late Roman textile traditions continuing seamlessly into the early Islamic period.
Segmentum from a Tunic
Egypt, Abbasid period · 750–99 · tapestry; linen and wool
Mezquita of Córdoba
Umayyad of al-Andalus · 784 CE onward
A forest of red-and-white striped arches from a great caliphate. A cathedral was dropped into the middle of it in the 16th century.
Fragment with Senmurvs
Iran or Iraq, in the style of the Abbasid period (750–1258) · prior to 1971 · Silk: lampas weave
Fragment with Equestrian Falconer King
Iran or Iraq, in the style of the Abbasid period (750–1258) · prior to 1971 · Silk: lampas weave
Enthroned King Flanked by Griffins
Iran or Iraq, in the style of the Abbasid period (750–1258) · prior to 1971 · Silk: lampas weave
Roundels with King Spearing Lion
Iran or Iraq, in the style of the Abbasid period (750–1258) · prior to 1971 · Silk: lampas weave
Imam Reza Shrine
Safavid → Pahlavi · 9th c. onward · Mashhad
The largest mosque in the world by floor area. Tomb of the eighth Shia Imam; seven courtyards; interiors covered in mirror mosaics that turn every surface into fractured gold.
Dish with Splashes of Green
Iraq, Basra, Abbasid period (750–1258) · 830–900 · Earthenware with overglaze design
Great Mosque of Kairouan
Aghlabid · 836 · Tunisia
The oldest purpose-built mosque still standing. Its square minaret became the model for North African and Andalusi mosques for a thousand years.
Great Mosque of Samarra
Abbasid · 851 · Iraq
Once the largest mosque in the world. Its Malwiya minaret spirals upward 170 feet — a ramp you climb on the outside.
Qarawiyyin Mosque
Idrisid · founded 859 · Fez
Founded by a woman — Fatima al-Fihri — in 859. Its attached madrasa may be the oldest continuously operating institution of higher learning in the world.
Mosque of Ibn Tulun
Tulunid · 879 · Cairo
The oldest mosque in Cairo still in its original form. A vast brick courtyard under a spiral minaret — a shape borrowed from Samarra.
Fragments with winged lions in roundels
Iran, Buyid or Seljuk period · 899–1160 (radiocarbon date, 95% probability) · Silk: taqueté
Blue Qur'an
Fatimid or Aghlabid · 9th–10th c. · North Africa
Gold Kufic script on indigo-dyed parchment — no other known Qur'an was copied on so dark a ground. Pages scattered across the world's museums; its origin still argued about.
Itchan Kala (Khiva)
Khanate of Khiva · 10th c. onward · Uzbekistan
The old inner town of Khiva — mud walls enclosing minarets, mausoleums, and palaces so untouched it can still look like a stage set.
Medina Azahara
Caliphate of Córdoba · 936–976 · Spain
The palace-city built outside Córdoba for Abd al-Rahman III — marble floors, mercury pools, gardens that moved. Destroyed in civil war within a century of completion.
Column Base
Spain, Cordoba, Spanish Umayyad period (756–1031) · c. 960–76 · Marble
Column Capital
Spain, Cordoba, Madinat al-Zahra, Spanish Umayyad period (756–1031) · c. 960–76 · Marble
Pyxis of al-Mughira
968 CE · Carved ivory · Cour Visconti
A small ivory box made in Córdoba for a teenage Umayyad prince. Every surface is carved with figures — musicians, lions, lovers, a man stealing eggs from an eagle's nest. The inscriptions include barely-veiled warnings about the instability of royal succession. Al-Mughira was executed by strangulation eight years later.
Fragments
by Egypt
Egypt · Arts of the Islamic World · 969–1171 · Linen and silk
Al-Azhar Mosque
Fatimid · 972 · Cairo
The mosque and university that became the intellectual center of the Sunni world. Continuously teaching since the tenth century.
Turban end with tiraz
Egypt, Tinnis, Fatimid period, datable to 983-90 · 983–990 · plain weave with inwoven tapestry weave: linen and silk
Weight
Egypt · 996-1021

1000 – 1500

Fragment with gold leaf lions
Iran or Iraq, Seljuk period · 1000s–1100s · Silk warp and cotton weft (mulham): plain weave; gold leaf, block printed
Fragment with falconer riding a bull in a rondel
Iran, Rayy, Seljuk period · 1000s–1100s (radiocarbon date, 95% probability) · Silk: double cloth
Embroidered border and field of animals in large roundels
Iraq, probably Baghdad, Seljuk period · 1000s–1100s · plain weave: silk warp and cotton weft (mulham): plain weave; silk and gold thread: embroidery, couched, split, running, and outline stitches:
Roundel with figure
Egypt, Fatimid period · 1000s · Linen and silk: plain weave with inwoven tapestry weave
Fragmentary roundel with eagle in gold ground
Egypt, Fatimid period · 1000s · linen, silk, and gold filé: plain weave with inwoven tapestry weave
Roundel with seated prince
Egypt, Fatimid period · 1000s · plain weave with inwoven tapestry weave: linen, silk, and gold filé
Tiraz Fragment
by Egypt
Egypt · Arts of the Islamic World · 1000–1100 · Linen
Qur'an of Ibn al-Bawwab
by Ibn al-Bawwab
Abbasid · 1000–1001 CE · Baghdad
The earliest surviving Qur'an in cursive naskh script — the style that would outlast the angular Kufic. Copied by one of Islam's greatest calligraphers in a single year.
Gumbad-e Qabus
Ziyarid · 1006 · Iran
A 53-meter brick tomb tower for Qabus ibn Wushmagir — a ten-pointed star plan tapering to a conical cap. No decoration beyond its geometry and its inscription band. The oldest surviving tower of its kind.
Al-Hakim Mosque
Fatimid · c. 1013 · Cairo
A vast early mosque named for a Fatimid caliph who vanished in the night, never found. Its stone minarets with bulbous caps are strange — unlike any other in the Fatimid world.
Tiraz with gold, probably from a wide sleeve
Egypt, Fatimid period, reign of Caliph al-Hakim, 1013–20 · 1013–1020 · plain weave with inwoven tapestry weave: linen, silk, and gold filé
Striped tiraz
Egypt, Fatimid period, reign of al-Mustansir · 1049–50 · plain weave with inwoven tapestry weave: linen and silk
Fatimid Luster Bowl with Antelope
Fatimid · 11th c. · Fustat (Old Cairo)
A ceramic bowl in metallic luster glaze — a single antelope at the center, stylized against a floral ground. Fatimid Egypt made luster ware a court art; the technique later migrated to Iran.
Fatimid Luster Bowl with Ibex
Fatimid · 11th c. · Fustat
A companion to the antelope bowl — an ibex, horns curling over its back. The Fustat kilns flourished for two centuries before a fire destroyed the quarter in 1168.
Fatimid Bowl
Fatimid · 11th c. · Fustat
A glazed ceramic bowl — plainer than the luster wares, but showing the wide painted-decoration tradition of Fatimid pottery. The base of the luxury market, not the top.
Fragment of a Tiraz
Egypt, Fatimid period, Caliphate of al-Mustansir, 1000s · 1058–60 · Linen and silk: tapestry (originally inwoven in a tabby ground)
Aljafería Palace
Taifa · 11th c. · Zaragoza
A fortified palace of the Banu Hud dynasty with a surviving Islamic core — interlaced polylobed arches, stucco arabesques. Now shares a building with the regional parliament.
Tiraz with gold
Egypt, Fatimid period · 1075–99 · plain weave with inwoven tapestry weave: linen, silk, and gold filé
Bab Zuwayla
Fatimid · 11th c. · Cairo
The southern gate of Fatimid Cairo — twin stone towers crowned by Mamluk-era minarets. A last public-execution site in the 19th century; heads on display above the gate into the 1800s.
Turban or shawl end with tiraz and gold
Egypt, Fatimid period, reign of al-Musta‘lī · 1094 · plain weave with inwoven tapestry weave: linen, silk, and gold filé
Mina'i Lobed Bowl with a Seated Prince and Mythical Creatures
by Iran
Iran · 1099–1299 · Mina'i ware
Bowl with Two Figures on Horseback
by Iran
Iran · rayy · 1099–1299 · Mina'i ware
Pierced Bowl by Hasan al-Qashani
by Hasan al-Qashani
Seljuq Iran · late 11th–early 12th c.
An openwork ceramic bowl of translucent white clay — molded so finely that liquid supposedly passed through but light did not. Signed by Hasan al-Qashani of Kashan.
Folio from a Qur'an
Iran, Seljuq period (1037–1194) · 1100s · ink, gold, and colors on paper
Page from a Qur'an, Sura al-Nisa 4, Verses 157–58
Seljuk Iran · 1100s · opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper
Double-Sided Leaf from a Qur'an
Seljuk Iran · 1100s · opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper
Page from a Qur'an, Sura al-Nisa 4, verses 159–60
Seljuk Iran · 1100s · ink, gold, and colors on paper
Bowl with Griffin
Northwest Iran, Garrus district, Seljuk period, 12th century · 1100s · earthenware with underglaze slip-painted decoration
Bowl with Calligraphic Decoration
by Iran
Iran · gabri · 1100–1300 · Garrus ware
Bracelet
Iran · 1100-1200
Tiraz with guilloche bands
Egypt, Fatimid period · 1100s · plain weave with inwoven tapestry weave: linen and silk
Shallow Bowl with Vegetal Decoration
by Iran or South Caucasus
Iran · gabri · 1101–1300 · Earthenware incised through a white slip and painted in green and brown under transparent glaze
Inscribed Tombstone of Shaikh al-Husain ibn Abdallah ibn al-Hasan (died 1110)
Iran, Yazd, Seljuk Period, early 12th Century · 1110 · limestone
Great Mosque of Tlemcen
Almoravid · 1136 · Algeria
An Almoravid foundation whose carved plaster muqarnas dome and minbar count among the most elaborate surviving examples of North African Berber craftsmanship. Its minaret was added under the Zayyanids two centuries later.
Kutubiyya Mosque
Almohad · 1150s · Marrakech
A red sandstone minaret 250 feet tall — the template for the Giralda of Seville and the Hassan Tower of Rabat. Named for the booksellers who clustered at its base.
Alcázar of Seville
Almohad → Mudéjar → Christian · 10th–14th c.
A royal palace begun by the Abbadids, expanded by Christian kings who kept the Islamic craftsmen — the result is Mudéjar: Moorish form under Castilian rule.
Great Mosque of Herat
Ghurid onward · 12th c. · Afghanistan
The Friday Mosque of Herat, its courtyard walls tiled in a dense cobalt and turquoise mosaic. Its tile workshops still operate beside the mosque, rebuilding what time erodes.
Khamsa of Nizami
Persian · 12th c. poem, illuminated 14th c. onward
Five epic romances — Khosrow and Shirin, Layla and Majnun, and others — illustrated by every Persian royal workshop for centuries. The British Library copy alone has 30 miniatures.
Merv
Seljuk · 11th–12th c. · Turkmenistan
A caravan oasis on the Silk Road — Alexander's Margiana, the Sasanian second city, a Seljuk capital. The mausoleum of Sultan Sanjar still stands, its blue dome long since fallen in.
Jug with Running Animals
Iran, probably Kashan, Seljuq period of Iran (1037–1194) · 1150–1220 · fritware with design in carved and underglaze-painted slip ("silhouette" ware)
Dish with a Seated Figure
by Iran
Iran · rayy · 1150–1250 · Lusterware
Luster Dish with Seated Prince
Iran, Kashan, Seljuq period of Iran (1037–1194) · 1170–1220 · fritware with luster-painted design
Luster Dish with Polo Player
Iran, Seljuq period (1037–1194) · 1170–1200 · fritware with luster-painted design
Jug
Iran, Rayy, Seljuk period · late 1100s · fritware with luster-painted design
Mina'i Beaker with Seated Princes
Iran, Kashan, Seljuq period of Iran (1037–1194) · 1180–1220 · Fritware with overglaze design, mina’i ware
Tankard
Iran · 1180-1220
Spouted jug
Iran · 1180-1220
Serving Bowl with Miniature Figures, Arabic Blessings, and Persian Poetry
by Iran
Iran · Islamic (culture or style) · 1186–1196 · Lusterware
Giralda
Almohad minaret → Christian bell tower · 1184–1198
The Almohads built it as a minaret; after the Reconquista it became a cathedral bell tower. A ramp inside let the muezzin ride up on horseback.
Koutoubia Mosque
Almohad · 1195 · Marrakech
A seventy-seven-meter minaret whose proportions became the model for Rabat's Hassan Tower and Seville's Giralda. Its red sandstone is visible from every part of old Marrakech; Almohad law forbade taller buildings within sight.
Hassan Tower
Almohad · 1195 · Rabat
An unfinished minaret meant to top the largest mosque in the western Islamic world — the caliph Yaqub al-Mansur died and work halted at forty-four meters, half its intended height. Rows of stumpy columns still mark where the prayer hall would have stood.
Seljuq Standing Figure
Seljuq Iran · 12th–13th c.
A glazed stucco courtier in a jeweled headdress and beaded collar — from a palace ensemble that lined a reception hall. A rare survival of figural Islamic architectural sculpture.
Fragment
Egypt, Mamluk sultanate (1250–1517) · 1200s–1300s · Block printing on plain weave cotton ground
Fragment of a Woodblock Print on Linen
Mamluk Sultanate (Egypt) · 1200s–1300s · pigment, linen
Bowl
Iran, Kashan, Seljuq period of Iran (1037–1194) · early 1200s · fritware with luster-painted design

Spain, Granada, Nasrid period · 1200–1325 · lampas: silk and gold thread

Fragment from the Dalmatic of San Valero
by Spain
Spain · Arts of the Islamic World · 1201–1300 · Silk and gilt-animal-substrate-wrapped silk; weft-faced four-color complementary weft plain weave with inner warps and areas of detached plain interlacing of inner warps with complementary ground wefts
Qutb Minar
Delhi Sultanate · 1192–1220 · Delhi
The tallest brick minaret in the world — 238 feet of red sandstone, carved with Qur'anic calligraphy as deeply as architecture.
Al-Jazari Automata
by Ismail al-Jazari
Artuqid · 1206 · Upper Mesopotamia
An illustrated manuscript of ingenious devices — water clocks, a peacock hand-washer, a drinking elephant clock with automata on its howdah. Al-Jazari's engineering drawings are as clear as modern blueprints.
Tile frieze
Kashan · ca. 1215
Maqamat al-Hariri
by Yahya al-Wasiti
Abbasid · al-Wasiti · 1237 · Baghdad
An illustrated manuscript of Hariri's picaresque stories — streets, markets, pilgrims, drunks, courts of law. The earliest surviving paintings of daily Baghdad life.
Bowl with Tree, Fishpond, and Animal Motifs
by Iran
Iran · sultanabad · 1250–1350 · Lusterware
Star-Shaped Tile with Phoenix
by Iran
Iran · veramin · 1250–1290 · Lusterware

by Iran
Iran · 1250–1350 · Fritware with molded decoration
Woven silk and gold
Spain · 1250-1274
Star-Shaped Tile with Qur'anic Inscriptions
by Iran
Iran · 1256–1353 · Lusterware
Kashan Luster Tile with a Couple
Ilkhanid · Kashan · 1266
A star-shaped luster tile with an embracing couple at its center — a scene from Khosrow and Shirin or a similar Persian love story. Once part of a tile facade on a Sufi tomb.
Mamluk Enameled Bottle
Mamluk · late 13th c.
Blown glass enameled in ruby, cobalt, and gold with Arabic inscriptions and arabesques — the same technique used for mosque lamps, scaled to a slender bottle.
Lampas with phoenixes amid undulating vines
Eastern Iran, Ilkhanid period · late 1200s-1300s · lampas: silk, cotton, and gold thread
Bowl with Birds
by Iran
Iran · sultanabad · 1281–1349 · Fritware painted with gray and white slips under a transparent glaze
Ilkhanid Bowl with Flying Birds
Ilkhanid · Kashan · late 13th c.
A stonepaste bowl with cobalt-painted birds in flight around the rim. Kashan was the Ilkhanid pottery capital — mass-produced luster wares for courtly consumers.
Cap with Striped Inscribed Silk
Mamluk Sultanate (Egypt or Syria) · 1300s · Silk
Qualities of Ringdoves (recto); On the Benefits of Quails (verso), from a Persian Manafi‘ al-Hayawan (The Benefits of Animals) of Abu Said Ubaid-Allah ibn Jibrail ibn Bakhtishu (died 1058–68)
Iran, Maragah, Ilkhanid period (1256–1353) · c. 1300 · opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper
Folio from a Persian Manafi‘ al-Hayawan (The Benefits of Animals) of Abu Said Ubaid-Allah ibn Jibrail ibn Bakhtishu (died 1058–68) (recto)
Iran, Maragah, Ilkhanid period (1256–1353) · c. 1300 · opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper
Panel
Egypt · 1296
Alhambra
Nasrid dynasty · 13th–14th c. · Granada, Spain
A palace-citadel of courtyards, fountains, and muqarnas ceilings. The poems inscribed on the walls are part of the architecture.
Generalife Gardens
Nasrid · 13th–14th c. · Granada
The summer gardens of the Nasrid sultans — water channels running the length of the courtyard, fountains cooling long porticoes. Paradise as hydraulic engineering.
Qur'an Manuscript Folio
Egypt, Mamluk sultanate (1250–1517) · 1300s · ink, gold, and colors on paper

Iran · 1301–1400 · Ink and opaque watercolor on paper
Balustrade Fragment with Animals from the residence of Hajji Hasan ibn Ibrahim ibn Muhammad
Iran, Hamadan, Ilkhanid period (1256–1353) · 1303–4 · limestone
Dome of Soltaniyeh
Ilkhanid Mongol · 1302–1312 · Iran
Second only to Hagia Sophia's in size when built. A blue-tiled double-shell dome soaring over the tomb of the Ilkhanid ruler Oljeitu — centuries ahead of its engineering.
Peacock-shaped Hand Washing Device (recto); Text Page, Arabic Prose (verso)
Syria, Damascus, Mamluk Period, 14th Century · 1315 · opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Peacock-shaped Hand Washing Device: Illustration from The Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices (Automata) of Inb al-Razza al-Jazari (recto)
Syria, Damascus, Mamluk Period, 14th Century · 1315 · opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Al-Attarine Madrasa
Marinid · 1325 · Fez
A tiny college next to the spice market whose four walls are totally covered in carved plaster, cedar, and zellij. Every square inch repays close looking: calligraphic friezes in Kufic and Thuluth, muqarnas capitals, and a delicately fountained courtyard only seven meters across.
Mamluk Minbar Doors
Mamluk Egypt · c. 1325
A pair of wooden doors inlaid with ivory and ebony in tight geometric stars — each star a polygonal meditation, complex enough to have inspired modern tessellation theory.
Baptistery of Saint Louis
c. 1320–1340 · Hammered brass inlaid with silver and gold · Cour Visconti
A Mamluk basin made in Egypt or Syria, covered in inlaid scenes of hunters, courtiers, and animals. At some point it entered the French royal treasury, and by tradition — probably invented — it was used for the baptism of royal children, including the future Louis XIII.
Bahram Gur Slays a Dragon
Ilkhanid · 1330s · Tabriz
A folio from the Great Mongol Shahnama — the hero-king drives a spear through a coiling dragon. Made for a Mongol court already patronizing Persian epic as their own.
Bahram Gur Arrives at the House of a Merchant, text page (recto), from a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (940–1019 or 1025), known as the Great Mongol Shahnama
Iran, Tabriz, Ilkhanid period (1256-1353) · 1330–35 · opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper
Bahram Gur Arrives at the House of a Merchant, text page (recto); Bahram Gur Slays a Dragon (verso), from a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (940–1019 or 1025), known as the Great Mongol Shahnama
Iran, Tabriz, Ilkhanid period (1256-1353) · 1330–35 · opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper
Portrait of Nushirwan the Just (verso) from a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (940–1019 or 1025)
Iran, Tabriz, Ilkhanid period (1256-1353) · 1330–35 · opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper
Persian Verses: The Fortieth Year of King Kisra Nushirwan's Reign and the Story of Buzurgmihr (recto); Portrait of Nushirwan the Just (verso) from a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (940–1019 or 1025)
Iran, Tabriz, Ilkhanid period (1256-1353) · 1330–35 · opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper
Persian Verses: The Fortieth Year of King Kisra Nushirwan's Reign and the Story of Buzurgmihr (recto) from a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (940–1019 or 1025)
Iran, Tabriz, Ilkhanid period (1256-1353) · 1330–35 · Ink and opaque watercolor on paper
[Juz 30 from the Qur'an]
Iran · ca.1330-1340
Mamluk Mosque Lamp
Mamluk · 13th–14th c. · Egypt and Syria
Blown glass enameled with gold, lines of Qur'anic inscription winding around the body. Lit, they cast filigreed shadows on the walls of vast Mamluk mosque interiors.
Animals, Birds, and Plants (verso) from a Mu'nis al-Ahrar fi Daqa'iq al-Ash'ar (The Free Men's Companion to the Subtleties of Poems) of Muhammad Ibn Badr al-Din Jajarmi (active 1340s)
Iran, Shiraz, Ilkhanid period (1256-1353) · c. 1341 · opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper
Alhambra Palace Silk Curtain
Spain, Granada, Nasrid period · mid-1300s · silk; lampas weave
Po-i-Kalyan
Timurid · 12th–16th c. · Bukhara
A minaret, mosque, and madrasa across one square. Tradition says Genghis Khan spared the minaret because his hat fell off as he looked up at it.
Shah Cheragh
Qajar · 14th c. onward · Shiraz
A Shia shrine covered inside floor to ceiling in thousands of shards of mirror. Candlelight becomes a galaxy. Pilgrims hold a finger against the wall and watch their reflection splinter.
Bou Inania Madrasa
Marinid · 1350–1355 · Fez
A teaching mosque of carved cedar, zellige tile, and stucco so fine it looks punched through paper. The Marinid sultan reportedly said the building cost was so beautiful it didn't matter what it cost.
Jameh Mosque of Yazd
Azari style · 12th–15th c. · Iran
A tall twin-minaret mosque faced in blue tile mosaic. Its high entrance iwan is featured on the Iranian 200-rial note.
Alhambra Silk Curtain
Nasrid · 14th c. · Granada
A silk textile with Alhambra-style geometric and Kufic epigraphic panels — possibly hung in one of the palace's chambers. Granada under the last Muslim dynasty produced some of the finest Islamic textiles.
Mosque-Madrasa of Sultan Hasan
Mamluk · 1356–1363 · Cairo
A monumental stone box with a towering cruciform courtyard. One of the most audacious pieces of medieval Islamic architecture, by an unknown hand.
Woven silk
Granada · 1360-1420
Roundel
Granada (City) · 1370
Wine jar
Spain · ca. 1375-1425
Frieze panel
Spain · 1380-1420
Frieze
Spain · 1380-1420
Beam
Spain · 1380-1420
Fragment with Nasrid Coat of Arms
by Probably Emirate of Granada
Granada province · Arts of the Islamic World · 1395–1405 · Silk and gilt-animal-substrate-wrapped silk; satin weave with secondary binding warps and supplementary patterning wefts
Mausoleum of Khoja Ahmed Yasawi
Timurid · 1389–1405 · Turkistan, Kazakhstan
Commissioned by Timur for a Sufi poet's tomb. Left unfinished when Timur died; the scaffolding on the portal front is six centuries old.
The Story of Hushang (recto), Illustration and text (Persian Prose) from Majmac al-Tavarikh (A Compendium of Histories) of Hafiz-i Abru; Text Page, Persian Prose, (verso), from Majmac al-Tavarikh (A Compendium of Histories) of Hafiz-i Abru
Afghanistan, Herat, Timurid period (1370–1501) · early 1400s · ink and opaque watercolor on paper

Afghanistan, Herat, Timurid period (1370–1501) · c. 1425 · ink and opaque watercolor on paper

by Iran or Central Asia
Iran · Islamic (culture or style) · 1400–1500 · Jade with incised decoration
Striped Silk from a Garment
Spain, Granada, Nasrid period · 1400s · silk; lampas weave
Lampas with rampant lions, palmettes, and escutcheons
Spain, Granada, Nasrid period · 1400s · lampas: silk
Casket
Granada · 1400-1450
Bahram Gur Visits the Princess of India in the Black Pavilion (recto): Illustration and Text, Persian Verses, from a manuscript of the Khamsa of Nizami, Haft Paykar [Seven Portraits]
Iran, possibly Tabriz or Shiraz, Timurid Period, early 15th century · c. 1400–1410 · opaque watercolor and ink on paper
Text Page, Persian Verses (verso) Bahram Gur Visits the Princess of India: from a manuscript of the Khamsa of Nizami, Haft Paykar [Seven Portraits]
Iran, possibly Tabriz or Shiraz, Timurid Period, early 15th century · c. 1400–1410 · ink and opaque watercolor on paper
Silk Fragment
Spain, Mudejar, 15th century · 15th century · lampas weave, silk
Bibi-Khanym Mosque
Timurid · 1399–1404 · Samarkand
Timur commissioned it after sacking Delhi — the largest mosque in the Islamic world at the time. The main dome collapsed within decades; centuries later the Soviets rebuilt it.
Tile Window Grille
by Iran
Iran · 1401–1500 · Glazed fritware tiles cut and reassembled as a mosaic
Narrow Strip
by Spain
Spain · Arts of the Islamic World · 1401–1500 · Silk
Gur-e Amir
Timurid · 1403 · Samarkand
The tomb of Timur under a ribbed turquoise dome. The grave was opened in 1941 with a curse; Germany invaded the USSR three days later.
Text Page, Persian Prose (recto); The Story of Adam peace upon him, his Sons and Progeny (verso)
Afghanistan, Herat, Timurid period (1370–1501) · c. 1425 · ink and opaque watercolor on paper
Text Page, Persian Prose (recto) from a Manuscript of the Majma' al-Tavarikh (A Compendium of Histories) by Hafiz-i Abru:
Afghanistan, Herat, Timurid period (1370–1501) · c. 1425 · ink and opaque watercolor on paper
The Story of Adam, peace upon him, his Sons and Progeny, from a Jami al-tavarikh (Compendium of Chronicles) of Rashid al-din (verso)
Afghanistan, Herat, Timurid period (1370–1501) · c. 1425 · opaque watercolor and ink on paper
The Story of Hushang, from a Majma al-tavarikh (A Compendium of Histories) of Hafiz-i Abru (recto)
Afghanistan, Herat, Timurid period (1370–1501) · 1425–50 · opaque watercolor and ink on paper
Ulugh Beg Observatory
Timurid · 1420s · Samarkand
A vast sextant sunk into the ground to measure stellar positions. The Zīj-i Sulṭānī compiled here tabulated over 1,000 stars — unsurpassed in accuracy until Tycho Brahe.
Shamsa (recto) from right folio from a double-page frontispiece of a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (940–1019 or 1025)
Iran, Shiraz, Timurid period (1370-1501) · c. 1444 · opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Timurid Royal Reception
Timurid · Shiraz · 1444
A double-folio frontispiece to a Shahnama — the royal court in a walled garden, a king receiving courtiers beneath a golden canopy. The Timurid workshops brought Persian painting to its quiet peak.
Royal Reception in a Landscape, right folio from a double-page frontispiece of a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (940–1019 or 1025)
Iran, Shiraz, Timurid period (1370-1501) · 1444 · opaque watercolor, gold, and silver on paper
Royal Reception in a Landscape, left folio from the double frontispiece of a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (Persian, about 940–1019 or 1025)
Iran, Shiraz, Timurid period (1370-1501) · 1444 · Opaque watercolor, ink, gold, and silver on paper
Royal Reception in a Landscape, right folio from the double frontispiece of a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (Persian, about 940–1019 or 1025) (verso)
Iran, Shiraz, Timurid period (1370-1501) · 1444 · opaque watercolor, gold, and silver on paper
Shah-i-Zinda
Timurid · 11th–19th c. · Samarkand
A necropolis of tiled mausoleums along a single lane. The tomb complex for the cousin of the Prophet — and for every Timurid noble who wanted to lie near him.
Bowl with Geometric Designs
Syria, Damascus, Burji Mamluk period, 15th Century · 1450–1500 · sheet brass, inlaid with silver and gold
Calligraphy, Persian Verses (verso)
Iran, Timurid period (1370-1501) · 1400s · Opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper

by Iran or Central Asia

by Iran

Iran · ca. 1450-1500
Chasuble
Egypt · ca. 1450-1500
Spanish Carpet with a Turkish Pattern
Spain, Alcaraz?, Mudejar, 15th century · c. 1450–1500 · wool, knotted pile: Spanish knot
Eyüp Sultan Mosque
Ottoman · 1458 (rebuilt 1800) · Istanbul
Built where the companion of Muhammad, Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, was said to have died during the 7th-century Arab siege of Constantinople. Ottoman sultans were girded with the sword of Osman at his tomb; the courtyard is still a pilgrimage site across the Sunni world.
Illuminated Folio (recto) from a Gulistan (Rose Garden) of Sa'di (c. 1213–1291)
by Sultan 'Ali al-Mashadi
Afghanistan, Herat, Timurid period (1370–1501) · c. 1475–1500; border: c. 1550 · Gum tempera, ink, gold, and silver on paper
Turban Helmet
by Eastern Turkey or Western Iran
Western Iran · Arts of the Islamic World · 1465–1510 · Steel and silver
Al Burdah
Egypt · ca. 1468-96
Chefchaouen
Moroccan · 15th c. onward
A mountain town in the Rif washed entirely in blue. Founded as a refuge for Moors and Jews expelled from Spain; the distinct blue wash is claimed variously as anti-mosquito, anti-evil-eye, or for keeping cool.
Tiled Kiosk
Ottoman · 1472 · Topkapı, Istanbul
Mehmed II's pavilion inside the first courtyard of Topkapı, used for watching polo matches. Its façade of star-and-hexagon tiles in cuerda seca and its blue-and-white mosaic entrance are direct echoes of Persian and Central Asian craft recruited to the new Ottoman capital.
Left side of a double-page album folio: Outside a Royal Encampment (recto) and Calligraphy, Persian Verses (verso)
by Bihzad

by Iran
Iran · 1486 · Lusterware
Tomb of Bibi Jawindi
Delhi Sultanate · 1493 · Uch Sharif, Pakistan
An octagonal blue-tiled tomb whose eastern half collapsed in a nineteenth-century flood. What survives — Multani-style glazed brickwork in lapis, turquoise, and white on red — is among the finest examples of pre-Mughal funerary architecture in the subcontinent.
The Constellation Leo, folio probably from the Kitab suwar al-kawakib al-thabita (Book of the Images of the Fixed Stars)

Piri Reis Map
by Piri Reis
Ottoman · Piri Reis · 1513
Surviving fragment of a world map drawn by the Ottoman admiral Piri Reis — the Atlantic coast of the New World only two decades after Columbus, drawn from prize charts captured in naval battle.
Page from a Shah-nama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (Persian, about 934–1020)
by Mir Musavvir
Iran, Tabriz, Safavid period (1501-1722) · 1520–37 · Gum tempera, ink, gold, and silver on paper

Velvet Fragment, Khusrau Sees Shirin Bathing
Iran, Kashan, Safavid period (1501-1722) · 1550–99 · silk; velvet, cut, pile-warp substitution
Rustam Slays the White Div
by Abd al-Vahhab
Shah Tahmasp Shahnameh · Safavid Tabriz · 1530s
A leaf from Shah Tahmasp's Shahnameh: Rustam reaching into the cave to split the White Demon with a dagger. This manuscript was the grandest Persian painting ever made; Tahmasp later gave it to Sultan Selim II.
Plate
Iznik · circa 1535-40
King Anushirwan and the Owls, from the Khamsa of Nizami
by Iran
Iran · saffarid · 1535 · Ink
Ardabil Carpet
Safavid Persia · 1539–1540 · Iran
Medallion carpet with 25 million knots. Made as a pair — the other half sold off piece by piece to repair this one. Now in the V&A.
The Ardabil Carpet
Iran · 1539-1540
Iznik Dish with Artichokes
Ottoman Iznik · c. 1540 · fritware
A dish painted with heavy cobalt artichokes and vines — early Iznik still imitated the Ming blue-and-white that Ottoman merchants brought in from Chinese ports.
An Episode from the Story of the Sasanian King Khusrau and His Beloved Shirin, from a Khamsa (Quintet) of Nizami (1141–1209) (verso)
Iran, probably Shiraz, Safavid period (1501-1722) · 1540–70 · opaque watercolor, ink, gold, and silver on paper
An Episode from the Story of the Sasanian King Khusrau and His Beloved Shirin, from a Khamsa (Quintet) of Nizami (1141–1209) (verso); Persian verses from a Haft Awrang (Seven Thrones) of Jami (d. 1492) (recto)
Iran, probably Shiraz, Safavid period (1501-1722) · 1540–70 · opaque watercolor, gold, silver, and ink on paper; double-sided
Calligraphy from a Ghazal of Badr al-Din Hilali Jaghata’i (Persian, active c. 1500)
by Faqir Ali
Iran, Safavid period (1501–1722) · c. 1550; borders added c. 1700s and mounted upside down · ink on paper (verso)
Kay Khosrow Crosses the Sea of Zareh on His Way to China, a scene from the Shahnama of Firdausi
by Iran
Iran · Islamic (culture or style) · 1545–1555 · Watercolor and ink on paper
Şehzade Mosque
by Mimar Sinan
Ottoman · 1548 · Istanbul
Sinan's first imperial commission, built for Suleiman the Magnificent in memory of his eldest son Şehzade Mehmed. The architect later dismissed it as an apprentice work — in fact it established the centralized four-semi-dome plan he would perfect at Selimiye.
Registan of Samarkand
Timurid · 15th–17th c. · Uzbekistan
Three madrasas framing a public square — Ulugh Beg's was home to one of the greatest astronomical observatories of the medieval world.
İznik Pottery
Ottoman · 15th–17th c. · Anatolia
The workshop town that supplied tiles for mosques from Istanbul to Damascus. The signature tomato-red slip was a short-lived chemical triumph.
Shahnameh of Shah Tahmasp
Safavid · 16th c. · Iran
A royal copy of Ferdowsi's epic with 258 miniatures painted by the court workshop — mountains with gold streams, demons in the rocks. One of the great illuminated manuscripts.
Ottoman Silk Kaftan Fragment
Ottoman Istanbul · 16th c.
A swatch of silver-brocaded silk in foliate medallions — from a ceremonial kaftan. Sultan's robes were preserved intact in Topkapı; fragments like this were cut from lesser garments.
Iznik Tile with Saz Leaves
Ottoman Iznik · c. 1550
A stonepaste tile painted in the "saz" style — long feathery leaves curling against a white ground. Cut by the thousand to panel Süleyman's mosques; the signature of Ottoman decoration.
Nushirwan Listens to the Owls (recto); the Story of Nushirwan and his Minister, "The Third Discourse on Diverse Events and Disorder in Life" (verso) from a Khamsa (Quintet) of Nizami (1141–1209)

Turkey, Istanbul or Bursa · 1550–75 · Silk and gilt-metal thread; lampas
Süleymaniye Mosque
Ottoman · Sinan · 1550–1557 · Istanbul
Mimar Sinan's masterpiece from the middle of his career — the architect's own tomb is in the garden outside.
Hunting with falcons in a landscape; verso: Calligraphy of Chaghatai Turkish poems in praise of wine, Sultan Muhammad Nur (Persian, c. 1472–1536) and Mirza Muhammad (probably Persian, active c. 1520s)
by Abd al-Samad
Mughal India, court of Akbar (reigned 1556–1605) · c. 1558–60; borders added probably 1700s · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Nushirwan Listens to the Owls
Safavid Qazvin · 1550s · illustration to Nizami's Khamsa
King Anushirvan halts his ride to hear two owls debate — the moral: a bad ruler leaves so many ruined villages that the owls get rich on the dowries of their daughters.
The merchant’s daughter encounters a wolf and bandits on her way to meet the gardener in order to keep her promise, from a Tuti-nama (Tales of a Parrot): Twelfth Night

The king plucks fruit from the Tree of Life with his own hands and feeds it to a lady, from a Tuti-nama (Tales of a Parrot): Ninth Night

The bag of gold which he received for the slave girl being stolen in a mosque, the young man of Baghdad tears his cloths and is about to fling himself into the Tigris, from a Tuti-nama (Tales of a Parrot): Forty-eighth Night
Mughal India, court of Akbar (reigned 1556–1605) · c. 1560 · gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Yusuf and Zulaykha (recto); Text Page, Persian Verses (verso)
Iran, Shiraz or Mashhad, Safavid period (1501–1722) · c. 1556–65 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Safavid Silk Animal Carpet
Safavid Iran · 16th c.
Silk pile with tigers, dragons, and simurghs in a flowering medallion field. Safavid court carpets were so coveted in Europe that Titian and Holbein painted them under Madonnas.
Khujasta and the Parrot
Mughal India · Akbar's Tutinameh · c. 1560
The merchant's wife Khujasta about to slip out for a night with a lover; the parrot begins the first of seven nights of stalling tales to keep her home — Akbar's early masterpiece of narrative painting.
The Salting Carpet
Iran · 1560-1580
Rüstem Pasha Mosque
by Mimar Sinan
Ottoman · 1563 · Istanbul
Sinan designed this compact mosque for the grand vizier Rüstem Pasha above a row of shops, hiding the prayer hall on a raised platform. It is clad inside and out in the most lavish İznik tiles Sinan ever deployed — flowers bloom on every surface.
Ben Youssef Madrasa
Saadian · 1565 · Marrakech
Once the largest Quranic college in North Africa, housing up to 900 students in tiny cells around a central courtyard. Cedar, marble, and zellij tilework interlock in concentric geometric schemes calibrated for the hours of daylight prayer.
Agra Fort
Mughal · 1565–1573 · Uttar Pradesh
Red sandstone fort rebuilt by Akbar, later inner palaces added by Shah Jahan. Imprisoned here by his own son Aurangzeb, Shah Jahan could see the Taj Mahal across the river until his death.
Kashan Silk Carpet
Safavid · Kashan · 16th c.
A smaller prayer-format silk carpet on a ground of deep red, with a directed arch and two columns — a portable mihrab, woven at unusually high knot counts.
Iznik Tile Spandrel with Saz Sprays
Ottoman Iznik · c. 1570 · fritware with underglaze
A ceramic spandrel — the curving wedge above an arch — with cobalt tulips and saz leaves. Iznik in the 1570s was the workshop that tiled Süleymaniye in Istanbul and Selimiye in Edirne.
A Mughal courtier
Mughal India, court of Akbar (reigned 1556–1605) · c. 1575; border added probably 1700s · Gum tempera and gold on paper, mounted with gold-sprinkled borders
Tankard (Hanap) with Tulips, Hyacinths, Roses, and Carnations
by Turkey
Turkey · iznik · 1570–1599 · Iznik ware
Tile panel
Iznik · ca. 1570-1580
Sokollu Mehmed Pasha Mosque
by Mimar Sinan
Ottoman · Sinan · 1571 · Istanbul
One of Sinan's jewels — a small mosque on a sloping site, its mihrab wall studded with Iznik tiles and three authenticated stones from the Kaaba.
Sidi Saiyyed Mosque Screens
Gujarat Sultanate · 1573 · Ahmedabad
Ten pierced-stone jali windows of the tree-of-life pattern — a tamarind tree winding through palm fronds — rank among the most reproduced motifs of Indo-Islamic art. The Indian Institute of Management Ahmedabad adopted it as a logo.
Selimiye Mosque
Ottoman · Sinan · 1575 · Edirne
Sinan called it the masterpiece of his old age. Eight piers lift a dome wider than Hagia Sophia's — the height of Ottoman engineering.
The Prince and the Petitioner
by Vali Jan
Safavid · Vali Jan · late 16th c.
A court drawing in ink and gold — a young prince seated on a rug, a humble man approaching with a petition. Vali Jan drew courtly manners with a faint ironic smile.
Calligraphic exercises and verses of Hafiz (Persian, about 1325–1389) (verso)
by Mahmud ibn Ishaq al-Shahabi
Persian, Uzbekistan, Bukhara · 1575–76 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Persian Couplets (recto), Calligraphy, Persian Verses; Single Page Manuscript
Iran, Isfahan, Safavid period (1501-1722) · late 1500s–early 1600s · ink, gold, and opaque watercolor on paper
Bottle
Iznik · 1575-1580
Zal Climbing to Rudaba, page from a copy of the Shahnama of Firdausi
by Iran
Shiraz · safavid · 1575–1590 · Opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Spandrel
Iznik · ca. 1575
Lampas with scenes of wild animals
Iran, Safavid period (1501–1722) · late 1500s–early 1600s · Silk and gilt-metal thread: lampas, brocaded
Saadian Tombs
Saadian · 1578 · Marrakech
Lost behind a high wall after the Alaouite sultan Moulay Ismail sealed their entrance, then rediscovered from aerial photos in 1917. The Hall of Twelve Columns — gilded muqarnas vaults over sixty-six princely graves in white Carrara marble — is among the finest interiors in Morocco.
Gilded Dish with Flowers and Leaves
Turkey, Iznik, Ottoman period (1299–1922) · c. 1590 · Fritware with red slip, underglaze design, and overglaze gilding
Bath Pail (Satl)
Iran, Isfahan, Safavid period (1501-1722) · c. 1580–1610 · cast brass, turned, engraved, inlaid with black compound (niello)
A royal ram with a gold chain
India, Mughal · c. 1585; border added probably 1700s · Gum tempera and gold on paper
Prince Riding in Chariot Drawn by Goats
by India
India · 1580–1725 · Opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Shishak
by Ottoman, Turkish
Turkey · Arts of the Islamic World · 1580–1620 · Gilded copper
Mosque Lamp
Turkey, Iznik, Ottoman period (1299–1922) · 1585–95 · Fritware with underglaze design
Kichik Beg Wounded during Babur's Attack on Qalat, from a copy of the Baburnama (Book of Babur)
by India
India · mughal · 1585–1595 · Opaque watercolor and gold on paper
The Young Emperor Akbar Arrests the Insolent Shah Abu’l-Maali, Page from a Manuscript of the Akbarnama
by India
India · Islamic (culture or style) · 1585–1595 · Opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Al-Mu'tazz Sends Gifts to Abdulla ibn Abdulla, from a copy of the Tarikh-i Alfi
by India
India · South Asian · 1587–1599 · Opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Rustam meets the challenge of Ashkabus, from a Shah-nama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (Persian, about 934–1020)
Iran, Shiraz, Safavid period (1501-1722) · c. 1590–1600 · opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Multi-necked vase
Iran · 1590-1640
Tarkhan Divana and Husain Quli Khan
by La'l
Mughal Empire · ca. 1590-95
Sultan Adam
by Tulsi
Mughal Empire · ca. 1590-95
Lotus-blossom petal canopy
Northern India · c. 1600 · silk: lampas
The siege of Arbela in the era of Hulagu Khan, from a Chingiz-nama (Book of Chingiz Khan) of the Jami al-tavarikh (Compendium of Chronicles) of Rashid al-Din (Persian, 1247–1318)
by Basavana
Mughal India, court of Akbar (reigned 1556–1605) · c. 1596 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Hulagu Khan giving a feast and dispensing favor upon the amirs and princes, from a Chingiz-nama (Book of Chingiz Khan) of the Jami al-tavarikh (Compendium of Chronicles)
by Lal
Mughal India · c. 1596–1600 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Portrait of Suraj Singh Rathor, Raja of Marwar and Maternal Uncle of Shah Jahan: A Page from the Prince Khurram Album
by Bishandas
India, Mughal court, early 17th century · c. 1600 · Gum tempera and gold on paper
The Ascent of the Prophet to Heaven, Page from the Khamsa of Nizami
by Iran
Iran · Islamic (culture or style) · 1595–1605 · Opaque watercolor
European Banquet Scene (or The Marriage Feast at Cana?)
by India
India · 1595–1605 · Opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Akbar supervising the capture of wild elephants at Malwa in 1564, painting 90 from an Akbar-nama (Book of Akbar) of Abu’l Fazl (Indian 1551–1602)
by Farukh Chela
Mughal India · c. 1602–3; borders added c. 1700s · ink with use of colors and gold on paper, mounted on an album page with borders of gold-decorated buff and blue paper (recto); calligraphy by Faqir Ali (verso)
Naqsh-e Jahan Square
Safavid · 1598 onward · Isfahan
One of the largest squares in the world. Built by Shah Abbas to stage polo, military reviews, and the stateliness of a capital — framed on four sides by a mosque, a palace, a bazaar, and a private chapel.
Yaja and Upayaja perform a sacrifice for the emergence of Dhrishtadyumna from the fire, from Adi-parva (volume one) of the Razm-nama (Book of Wars) adapted and translated into Persian by Mir Ghiyath al-Din Ali Qazvini, known as Naqib Khan (Persian, d. 1614) from the Sanskrit Mahabharata
by Bilal Habshi
Mughal India, made for Abd al-Rahim the Khan Khanan · 1598 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Timur distributes gifts from his grandson, the Prince of Multan, from a Zafar-nama (Book of Victories)
by Shravana
Mughal India, court of Akbar (reigned 1556–1605) · 1598–1600 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Spandrel with a Hunting Scene
by Iran
Iran · safavid · 1599–1699 · Fritware with polychrome overglaze decoration
Portrait of an Artist or Scholar
by India
India · 1599–1699 · Watercolor
Ali Qapu
Safavid · early 17th c. · Isfahan
Six-story royal pavilion overlooking Naqsh-e Jahan Square. Its upper music room has plaster cut into silhouettes of jars and vases — both ornament and acoustic resonator.
Itchan Kala View
Khanate of Khiva · 10th c. onward · Uzbekistan
The walled inner town of Khiva — minarets, mausoleums, madrasas all packed inside dun-colored mud-brick walls. So intact it can feel like a stage set.
The Concourse of the Birds
by Habiballah of Sava
Safavid · Habiballah of Sava · c. 1600
A single folio from the Mantiq al-Tayr — Attar's Sufi parable of birds that fly to seek their king and discover they are he. Painted by a master of the Isfahan school.
Ottoman Christ-Medallion Lampas
Ottoman Bursa · 16th–17th c.
A silk-and-gilt lampas woven with roundels showing Christ in benediction — an Ottoman weaver's product for a Christian client within the Empire, presumably a liturgical vestment.
Wall Tiles
Turkey (Iznik), Ottoman Period · 1600s · fritware with underglaze-painted design
Wall Tile
Turkey (Iznik), Ottoman Period · 1600s · fritware with underglaze-painted design
Royal Carpet with Silk and Metal Thread

by Riza-yi Abbasi
Iran, Isfahan, Safavid period (1501-1722) · early 1600s · opaque watercolor and gold on paper

by India

Four Tiles with a Figural Scene
by Iran
Iran · 1601–1700 · Fritware with polychrom overglaze decoration
Reza Abbasi Paintings
by Reza Abbasi
Safavid · c. 1600
The defining painter of the late Safavid court — languid youths, calligraphic lines, single figures filling the page. He turned Persian miniature away from narrative tableaux toward portraiture.
Portrait of Sultan Ahmet I
Ottoman · early 17th c.
A painted profile of Ahmet I, builder of the Blue Mosque — rendered in small miniature scale with the canonical turban and sable-lined robe. One of an album of sultan-portraits.
Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed)
Ottoman · 1609–1616 · Istanbul
The Ottoman answer to Hagia Sophia across the square. More than 20,000 İznik tiles line the interior — the blue that gave it its Western name.
Jahangir's jade wine cup
by Sa'ida-ye Gilani
Mughal empire · 1613
Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque
Safavid · 1603–1619 · Isfahan
The private royal chapel on the east side of Naqsh-e Jahan Square. No minarets, no courtyard — just a single domed hall whose tilework, buff and turquoise, changes color with the sun.
From Dohras (Songs) 40 and 42 from the Kitab-i Nauras (Book of Nine Essences) of Sultan Ibrahim Adil Shah II of Bijapur (r. 1580–1627); verso: From Dohras (Songs) 40 and 36 from the Kitab-i Nauras of Sultan Ibrahim Adil Shah II
by Khalilullah Butshikan
India, Bijapur, Deccan · 1618 · ink with two-tone gold on paper, double-sided
Portrait of Raja Jagat Singh of Nurpur
by Bichitr
Mughal India · Bichitr · c. 1619
A portrait by Bichitr of the Punjab Hills raja — a vassal ruler who would later rebel against Jahangir. Bichitr, signing his name in the lower margin, was Jahangir's most psychologically acute painter.
Shah Mosque, Isfahan
Safavid Persia · 1611–1629 · Iran
Seven-colour tilework on the scale of a whole square. Inside the dome, one clap echoes seven times.
Lotto Carpet
Ottoman · Ushak · 17th c.
A wool pile carpet with a distinctive yellow arabesque on red — the "Lotto pattern," named for the paintings in which Lorenzo Lotto laid them under his Madonnas. Widely exported to Europe.
Mother and Child
by India
India · 1620–1630 · Black ink with gold on paper
Prince Visiting an Ascetic during a Hunt
by India
India · Islamic (culture or style) · 1620–1655 · Opaque watercolor
Nur Jahan holding a portrait of Emperor Jahangir
by Bishandas
Mughal India · c. 1627 · gum tempera and gold on paper
Tomb of I'timād-ud-Daulah
Mughal · 1622–1628 · Agra
"The Baby Taj" — the first Mughal tomb clad entirely in white marble and inlaid with pietra dura. Built by Nur Jahan for her father; the stylistic prototype for the Taj Mahal eight years later.
Prince and ascetics, from the Late Shah Jahan Album
by Govardhan
India, Mughal court, reign of Shah Jahan (1628-1658), Mughal Dynasty (1526-1756) · c. 1630 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
A Procession Scene with Musicians, from a copy of the Padshanama
by India
India · South Asian · 1625–1675 · Opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Velvet panel with four youths smelling flowers
Iran, Isfahan or Yazd, Safavid period (1501-1722) · 1625–50 · Silk: velvet, brocaded, and pile-warp substitution; gilt- and silver-metal thread
Lovers Embracing
Mughal India · c. 1630 · Gum tempera and gold on paper
Muhammad Ali Beg
by Hashim
Mughal Empire · ca. 1631
Shah Jahan's thumb ring
India · 1632
An African Lyre Player (recto)
India, Deccan, 17th century · c. 1640–60 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Tomb of Jahangir
Mughal · 1637 · Lahore
Jahangir — Akbar's son, Shah Jahan's father — lies under white marble inlaid with pietra dura flowers, in a garden tomb of red sandstone. The minarets at the corners rise but never touch the roof.
Beauty Holding a Flower
by India
India · 1640–1660 · Opaque watercolor
Shalamar Gardens
Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1641 · Lahore
Three terraces of Persian-style paradise garden, 400 fountains fed by a canal that once ran from the Himalayas. Listed in danger after colonial-era road-widening destroyed the outer walls.
Wazir Khan Mosque
Mughal · 1641 · Lahore
A small neighborhood mosque entirely encrusted in Persian kashikari tilework whose floral panels are unmatched in subcontinental Islamic art. Commissioned by Shah Jahan's governor of Punjab, it is the premier showcase of seventeenth-century Mughal ceramic craft.
Chehel Sotoun
Safavid · 1647 · Isfahan
A pavilion of "forty columns" — twenty real wooden ones plus their reflections in the long rectangular pool. Interior halls are painted with the Shah's battles and banquets.
Shah Jahan Mosque, Thatta
Mughal · 1647 · Sindh, Pakistan
Shah Jahan built this mosque in thanks for the town's shelter after a revolt. Ninety-three domes and thirty-three arches are said to amplify the imam's voice so the entire congregation can hear without microphones — a feat of Mughal acoustical engineering.
Harem night-bathing scene, from the Late Shah Jahan Album (recto)
Mughal India, court of Shah Jahan (reigned 1628–58) · c. 1653 · Gum tempera, gold, and ink on paper
Tughra: Shah Muhammad bin Ibrahim Khan, al-muzaffar daima (Sultan Mehmed, son of Ibrahim Khan, the eternally victorious; Calligraphy (Ottoman Turkish Signature), Single Page Manuscript
Turkey, Ottoman period, reign of Sultan Mehmed IV, 1648–87) · 1648–87 · opaque watercolor, gold, and silver on paper
Topkapı Palace
Ottoman · 15th–19th c. · Istanbul
The sultans' residence for 400 years. Unlike European palaces, it's a scatter of pavilions in a walled garden — closer to a fortified village.
Royal Horse Inspection
by Iran, probably Isfahan
Iran · safavid · 1650–1675 · Opaque watercolor and gold on paper

India · South Asian · 1667–1700 · Opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Badshahi Mosque
Mughal · 1673 · Lahore
Aurangzeb's great imperial mosque, faced with red sandstone and inlaid with white marble. Its courtyard holds 100,000 worshippers; for 313 years it was the largest mosque in the world.
Portrait of Raja Ram Singh of Amber (r. 1667-1688) with a Deccan Sword (recto); Calligraphy (verso)

by Iran
Iran · isfahan · 1701–1800 · Steel inlaid with gold with engraved and pierced decoration
Mausoleum of Moulay Ismail
Alaouite · late 17th century · Meknes
Burial place of the sultan who built seven hundred palaces and commanded an army of black guards numbering 150,000. The sanctuary is one of the few in Morocco non-Muslims may enter; its painted cedar ceiling and tilework glitter around four clocks presented by Louis XIV.
Damascus Room
Ottoman Syria · 1707
A complete wooden interior from an Ottoman-period Damascus merchant's house — painted panels, carved arches, a fountain floor. Dismantled and reinstalled at the Met; the everyday luxury of 18th-century Damascus.
Husain Ali Khan Entertaining His Brothers (The Sayyid Brothers)
India, Mughal Dynasty (1526-1756) · c. 1712–19 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Bab el-Mansour
Alaouite · 1732 · Meknes
An enormous ceremonial gate into the ruined imperial city of Moulay Ismail, flanked by columns from Volubilis — Roman marble recycled into Moroccan empire.
Oval portrait of a woman in a Chaghtai hat

Qajar Wall Cover with Peacocks
Qajar Iran · 19th c. · painted and printed silk
A silk hanging hand-painted with peacocks and European-style portrait medallions — late Qajar taste blended Persian ornament with imported chromolithograph imagery.

Iran · 1887-1888
Nasir al-Mulk Mosque
Qajar · 1888 · Shiraz
The "Pink Mosque" — stained-glass windows scatter colored light across Persian carpets every morning. Built late in the Islamic architectural tradition, as a kind of summary.
Bahia Palace
Late Alaouite · 1866–1900 · Marrakech
A palace for the grand vizier Ahmed ben Moussa and his four wives, its 150 rooms arranged around courtyards of green tilework, painted cedar ceilings, and carved plaster. The name means 'the brilliant' — a name intended to impress visitors upon arrival.

Morocco · 1970-1980
Faisal Mosque
Modern Islamic · 1986 · Islamabad
A Turkish architect's vision of a Bedouin tent rendered in reinforced concrete and granite. One of the largest mosques in the world; a sharp-edged rejection of dome-and-minaret convention.
Hassan II Mosque
Modern Moroccan · 1986–1993 · Casablanca
Built out over the Atlantic on the king's sixtieth birthday. Its minaret is 210 meters — the second-tallest in the world. The hall's retractable roof opens to the sky.

Morocco · 2009
